page: Amazon Web Services r4.4xlarge is a memory-optimized fourth-generation instance utilizing the Xen hypervisor. It features an x86_64 architecture powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. The instance provides 16 dedicated vCPUs across 8 physical cores with hyper-threading enabled. It is equipped with 122.0 GB of system memory, yielding 7.62 GB of RAM per core. There is no local storage or GPU hardware bundled with this instance, and it delivers a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear distinction between single-core and multi-core capabilities. Single-core performance is relatively low, with stress-ng, Geekbench, and bzip3 single-threaded decompression scoring in the bottom 10%. However, multi-core performance scales to average levels, as demonstrated by Passmark CPU, Geekbench multi-core, and stress-ng multi-core tests. Memory bandwidth tests show average read speeds of 622.2 GB/sec for small cached blocks and 58.1 GB/sec for large uncached blocks, though large block write bandwidth is weaker, falling in the bottom 25%.

Given its high memory-to-vCPU ratio and average multi-core processing capabilities, the r4.4xlarge is suited for memory-intensive workloads that do not require high single-thread performance. These include database operations, static web serving, and software compilation, which all achieved average benchmark ratings. Conversely, it is less suitable for single-threaded text processing or write-heavy memory operations. From a cost-efficiency perspective, this instance offers a dense memory allocation relative to its compute resources, making it a viable option for legacy memory-bound applications where modern high-frequency CPUs are not required.
description: Amazon Web Services r4.4xlarge is a memory-optimized instance featuring 16 dedicated vCPUs and 122.0 GB of RAM, powered by an Intel Xeon E5-2686 v4 processor running at 2.3 GHz. Built on the x86_64 architecture and managed via the Xen hypervisor, this instance does not include local storage or GPU resources, but provides a baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps. Benchmark data shows poor single-core performance, but average multi-core and memory read bandwidth capabilities. This resource profile makes the instance highly suitable for memory-intensive workloads such as database operations, static web serving, and software compilation, where massive memory capacity is required but single-threaded CPU speed is not the primary bottleneck. It offers a cost-effective option for memory-bound legacy applications.

tagline: A memory-optimized virtual instance featuring 122 GB of RAM and 16 dedicated vCPUs for memory-intensive database and web serving workloads.

122.0 GB of system memory optimized for memory-bound applications
